info.parenttitle: B3.2 Structural systems application and selection (HL only)

markdown:
## Structural Failure


<definition term="Structural Failure">Structural failure occurs when a product or structure **can no longer carry the load** it was designed for, often leading to **deformation or breakage**.</definition>

### Causes of Structural Failure


| Cause | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Overloading | Exceeding the maximum load capacity leads to permanent deformation or collapse. |
| Material Choice | Using materials that are too brittle, too soft, or prone to corrosion can reduce durability. |
| Inadequate Size | Thin or under-dimensioned parts may buckle or snap under stress. |
| Poor Shape/Design | Sharp corners, stress concentrations, or uneven load distribution can weaken a structure. |

![Warehouse rack collapes due to overloading](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/5842d4e6785874b131ba829d85ca737496ad5194-1200x800.png)

<callout type="note">* Structural failure is not always catastrophic. 
* It can also manifest as **cracks**, **deformations**, or other signs of **distress** that compromise the structure's integrity over time.</callout>

### Analysing Structural Failure


<definition term="Finite Element Analysis (FEA)">A computerised simulation method that breaks down a model into small elements to analyse how it responds to physical forces.</definition>

#### What FEA Shows


1. **Stress concentration areas** (often visualised with colour maps)
2. **Points of failure** under load
3. **Deformation behaviour** over time

#### Interpreting FEA Data


1. **Identify** areas of **high stress** or **deformation**.
2. **Compare** the **results** to the **material's limits** to **predict failure points**.

![Finite Element Analysis (FEA) of a turbine (myengineeringltd)](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/24ec8f1ab4d84d4e9286a7d5dbf9f4a164dfe02f-1920x1080.png)

<callout type="tip">* When analyzing FEA data, focus on areas with **stress concentrations** or **unexpected deformations**. 
* These are often the first indicators of potential failure.</callout>

### Real-World Examples of Structural Failure


#### **Tacoma Narrows Bridge (1940)**


  1. **Cause**: **Aerodynamic instability** due to **poor design**.
  2. **Lesson**: The **importance** of **considering dynamic forces** like **wind**. The bridge's oscillations in the wind were not anticipated, leading to its dramatic collapse.

![Tacoma Narrows Bridge collapse in 1940 (Britannica)](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/730daad7c03a24e66d73da0f214f742fe282ba6f-1057x865.png)

#### **Hyatt Regency Walkway (1981)**


  1. **Cause**: **Design change** that **doubled** the **load** on **support rods**.
  2. **Lesson**: The **critical role** of **thorough design review** and **testing**.

![Hyatt Regency Walkway collapse in 1981 (CNN)](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/53455e11b879c6a10dde25c5b7db2fd6a6b3e379-2500x1643.png)
